title: Deaths from volcanic eruptions
subtitle: Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.
caption:
Deaths from volcanic eruptions

Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.

The chart displays annual global deaths from volcanic eruptions from 1570 to 2024 using vertical bars scaled against a y-axis ranging from 0 to 60,000. The timeline reveals a distribution characterized by long periods of low mortality punctuated by distinct, high-magnitude spikes. Between 1570 and 1750, the majority of years show values near the baseline, with occasional bars reaching between 2,000 and 10,000 deaths. A cluster of activity appears in the late 18th century, where several bars rise between 5,000 and 15,000. The highest value on the chart occurs around 1815, where a single bar extends to the 60,000 mark. Following this peak, values return to lower levels until the late 19th century, which features two significant events: one bar reaching approximately 36,000 around 1883 and another exceeding 40,000 around 1902. The 20th century shows a return to generally lower figures, mostly remaining below 5,000, though a notable spike reaches roughly 23,000 around 1985. The period from 2000 to 2024 displays minimal activity, with bars consistently staying well below the 5,000 threshold.
